faqs.q: Can I get a home loan on ₹25,000 salary?
faqs.a: Yes, several banks including SBI, Bank of Baroda, and PNB approve home loans for applicants earning ₹25,000 per month. At this salary, you can expect eligibility of ₹12–18 Lakh depending on the bank's FOIR and interest rate.

faqs.q: Which bank gives home loan on lowest salary?
faqs.a: SBI and Bank of Baroda have some of the lowest minimum income requirements for home loans, starting from ₹15,000–₹20,000 per month. Actual eligibility depends on your credit score and existing EMIs.

faqs.q: How much home loan can I get on ₹20,000 salary?
faqs.a: On a ₹20,000 monthly salary with no existing EMIs, you can typically get a home loan of ₹8–12 Lakh at current interest rates with a 20-year tenure. Adding a co-applicant can increase this amount significantly.

faqs.q: How to increase home loan eligibility with low salary?
faqs.a: You can increase eligibility by: adding a working spouse or family member as co-applicant, clearing all existing loans and credit card dues, choosing a longer tenure (up to 30 years), and maintaining a CIBIL score above 750.

faqs.q: Is PMAY available for low salary home loan applicants?
faqs.a: Yes, Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY) offers interest subsidy of ₹2.35–₹2.67 Lakh for EWS (income up to ₹3 Lakh/year) and LIG (income up to ₹6 Lakh/year) categories. Most banks process PMAY-linked home loans.

faqs.q: Can I get a personal loan with 600 CIBIL score?
faqs.a: Yes, some NBFCs like Bajaj Finserv and Tata Capital may approve personal loans with a CIBIL score of 600–650. However, the interest rate will be higher (16–24% p.a.) compared to applicants with 750+ scores.

faqs.q: Which bank gives personal loan on lowest CIBIL score?
faqs.a: NBFCs are generally more flexible than banks. Bajaj Finserv, Tata Capital, and L&T Finance consider applicants with lower credit scores. Among banks, IDFC First Bank and IndusInd Bank may approve with scores around 650.

faqs.q: How to get a personal loan with bad credit history?
faqs.a: Options include: applying with NBFCs that have flexible criteria, taking a secured personal loan against FD or gold, applying with a co-applicant who has good credit, or getting a small-ticket loan to rebuild your score first.

faqs.q: What interest rate will I get with low CIBIL score?
faqs.a: With a CIBIL score of 600–650, expect personal loan interest rates of 16–24% p.a. This is significantly higher than the 10–12% offered to applicants with 750+ scores. Improving your score before applying can save lakhs in interest.

faqs.q: How long does it take to improve CIBIL score for a personal loan?
faqs.a: It typically takes 3–6 months of consistent on-time payments, low credit utilization (below 30%), and no new credit inquiries to see a meaningful improvement. Clearing overdue accounts can boost your score by 50–100 points.

faqs.q: What is the maximum LTV ratio for loan against property?

faqs.q: Is loan against property better than a personal loan?

faqs.q: What documents are needed for loan against property?

faqs.q: How long does LAP approval take?

faqs.q: How to check CIBIL score for free?
faqs.a: You can check your CIBIL score for free through: (1) CIBIL's official website (1 free report per year), (2) Your bank's net banking or mobile app (SBI YONO, HDFC, ICICI), (3) Third-party apps like Paytm, PhonePe, or BankBazaar.

faqs.q: What CIBIL score is needed for a loan?
faqs.a: Most banks require a minimum CIBIL score of 700 for personal and home loans. A score of 750+ gets you the best interest rates. For gold loans, the requirement may be as low as 575. NBFCs may approve loans with scores as low as 600–650.

faqs.q: Does checking CIBIL score reduce it?
faqs.a: No, checking your own CIBIL score (soft inquiry) does not reduce it. Only hard inquiries by banks when you apply for a loan or credit card can slightly reduce your score. Multiple hard inquiries in a short period can lower your score by 5–10 points.

faqs.q: How to improve CIBIL score quickly?
faqs.a: Pay all EMIs and credit card bills on time, keep credit utilization below 30%, avoid multiple loan applications in a short period, maintain a healthy mix of secured and unsecured loans, and dispute any errors in your CIBIL report.

faqs.q: How often is CIBIL score updated?
faqs.a: Banks report your credit data to CIBIL every 30–45 days. Your CIBIL score typically updates within 30–45 days of any change in your credit behavior like paying off a loan or missing an EMI.

faqs.q: What documents are required for a home loan?
faqs.a: Basic documents include: PAN card, Aadhaar card, salary slips (3 months), bank statements (6 months), Form 16/ITR (2 years), property documents (sale agreement, title deed), approved building plan, and passport-size photographs.

faqs.q: What additional documents do self-employed applicants need?
faqs.a: Self-employed applicants need: ITR for last 3 years, business registration/GST certificate, audited financial statements, profit & loss statement, balance sheet, and business bank statements for last 12 months.

faqs.q: What property documents are needed for a home loan?
faqs.a: Property documents include: sale agreement or allotment letter, title deed or conveyance deed, approved building plan, occupancy certificate (for ready property), land revenue receipts, encumbrance certificate, and NOC from housing society.

faqs.q: Can I apply for a home loan without ITR?
faqs.a: Most banks require ITR for home loan approval. However, some banks accept salary certificates, Form 16, or bank statements as alternative income proof for salaried employees. Self-employed applicants generally need ITR.

faqs.q: How long are home loan documents valid?
faqs.a: Salary slips and bank statements should be from the last 3–6 months. ITR copies should be from the last 2–3 financial years. Property documents should be current and clear of any legal disputes. Some banks may ask for updated documents if processing takes longer.

pages.title: How to Improve CIBIL Score — 10 Proven Tips (2026)
pages.description: Learn how to improve your CIBIL score fast with 10 actionable tips. Understand score ranges (300-900), common myths, and how long each improvement takes in 2026.
pages.keywords: how to improve cibil score, improve credit score india, increase cibil score, cibil score improvement tips 2026, boost cibil score fast
pages.heroSub: 10 proven, actionable tips to boost your CIBIL score and unlock better loan rates. Updated for 2026.

faqs.q: How long does it take to improve CIBIL score from 600 to 750?
faqs.a: Improving your CIBIL score from 600 to 750 typically takes 6–12 months of disciplined credit behaviour. Paying all EMIs and credit card bills on time, reducing credit utilization below 30%, and avoiding new hard inquiries are the fastest levers. Clearing any overdue or settled accounts can accelerate the process.

faqs.q: Does closing a credit card improve CIBIL score?
faqs.a: No, closing a credit card usually hurts your score. It reduces your total available credit limit, which increases your credit utilization ratio. It also shortens your credit history length. Instead of closing old cards, keep them active with small purchases and timely payments.

faqs.q: Can I improve my CIBIL score without a credit card?
faqs.a: Yes. You can build or improve your CIBIL score by taking a small secured loan (like a gold loan or loan against FD), paying all existing EMIs on time, and ensuring utility bill payments are reported to credit bureaus. A credit-builder loan from select NBFCs is another option.

faqs.q: How many points can CIBIL score increase in one month?
faqs.a: In a single month, your CIBIL score can increase by 10–30 points if you clear overdue payments, reduce credit card balances significantly, or get errors removed from your report. Large jumps of 50–100 points typically require 3–6 months of consistent effort.

faqs.q: Does checking my own CIBIL score lower it?
faqs.a: No. Checking your own CIBIL score is a soft inquiry and does not affect your score. Only hard inquiries made by banks or NBFCs when you apply for a loan or credit card can reduce your score by 5–10 points per inquiry.

guideContent.text: What Is a CIBIL Score?

A CIBIL score is a three-digit number between 300 and 900 assigned by TransUnion CIBIL, India's oldest credit bureau. Banks and NBFCs use this score to evaluate your creditworthiness before approving any loan or credit card application. A higher score signals lower risk to lenders, which translates into faster approvals, higher loan amounts, and lower interest rates.
CIBIL Score Ranges and What They Mean

Most banks consider 750 and above as the benchmark for premium loan offers. If your score is below 700, you may still get a loan from NBFCs, but at significantly higher interest rates. Anything below 600 makes it very difficult to secure unsecured credit.
10 Proven Tips to Improve Your CIBIL Score
1. Pay All EMIs and Credit Card Bills on Time

Payment history is the single most important factor, accounting for roughly 35% of your CIBIL score. Even one missed or late payment can drop your score by 50–100 points. Set up auto-debit or payment reminders to ensure you never miss a due date. Timeline: immediate impact within 30–45 days of consistent on-time payments.
2. Keep Credit Utilization Below 30%

Credit utilization ratio is the percentage of your total credit limit that you are using. If your credit card limit is ₹1 Lakh, try to keep outstanding balances below ₹30,000 at any point. High utilization signals credit hunger to bureaus. Timeline: 1–2 billing cycles (30–60 days).
3. Avoid Multiple Loan Applications in a Short Period

Every time you apply for a loan or credit card, the lender makes a hard inquiry on your CIBIL report. Multiple hard inquiries within a short span suggest financial distress and can reduce your score by 5–10 points each. Space out applications by at least 3–6 months. Timeline: hard inquiries stay on your report for 2 years, but their impact fades after 6–12 months.
4. Maintain a Healthy Credit Mix

CIBIL rewards borrowers who demonstrate the ability to handle different types of credit. A mix of secured loans (home loan, car loan) and unsecured credit (personal loan, credit card) shows responsible borrowing. Avoid having only unsecured credit in your profile. Timeline: 3–6 months to reflect positively.
5. Do Not Close Old Credit Cards

The length of your credit history matters. Older credit accounts contribute positively to your score. Closing an old credit card reduces your average credit age and also lowers your total available credit limit, increasing utilization. Keep old cards active with small periodic transactions. Timeline: impact is immediate upon closure.
6. Clear Overdue and Settled Accounts

If you have any accounts marked as overdue, settled, or written off, these are severe negative marks. Contact the lender and pay the full outstanding amount to get the status changed to closed. A settled account (where you paid less than the full amount) is almost as damaging as a default. Timeline: 30–60 days after clearance for the update to reflect.
7. Review Your CIBIL Report for Errors

Errors in your credit report are more common than you think. Wrong account details, duplicate entries, or incorrectly reported late payments can drag your score down unfairly. Download your free annual CIBIL report from myscore.cibil.com and dispute any inaccuracies. Timeline: CIBIL resolves disputes within 30 days.
8. Become an Authorized User on a Good Account

If a family member has an excellent credit history, ask them to add you as an authorized user on their credit card. Their positive payment history and low utilization will reflect on your report too, giving your score a boost. Timeline: 1–2 billing cycles (30–60 days).
9. Opt for a Secured Credit Card or Credit-Builder Loan

If you have no credit history or a very poor score, start with a secured credit card (backed by a fixed deposit) or a credit-builder loan. These products are designed to help you build a positive credit trail with minimal risk. Several banks and NBFCs offer secured cards with limits starting at ₹10,000–₹25,000. Timeline: 3–6 months of usage to see meaningful score improvement.
10. Limit Total Debt Relative to Income

Keep your total EMI obligations (including the new loan you are planning) below 40–50% of your monthly income. This is known as the Fixed Obligation to Income Ratio (FOIR). A lower FOIR signals to both lenders and credit bureaus that you are not over-leveraged. Timeline: ongoing discipline; impact builds over 3–6 months.
Common CIBIL Score Myths

items: Myth: Checking your own CIBIL score reduces it. Fact: Self-checks are soft inquiries and have zero impact.
Myth: Paying the minimum due on credit cards is enough. Fact: While it avoids a late-payment mark, carrying forward balances increases utilization and costs you heavy interest.
Myth: Higher income means higher CIBIL score. Fact: CIBIL does not consider your income. It only tracks your borrowing and repayment behaviour.

Myth: Closing a loan early always helps your score. Fact: Prepaying a loan is financially smart, but closing your only active loan removes your active credit line, which can temporarily dip your score.
Myth: CIBIL is the only credit bureau in India. Fact: There are four RBI-licensed bureaus — CIBIL (TransUnion), Experian, Equifax, and CRIF High Mark. Most banks primarily use CIBIL.

guideContent.text: How Long Does Each Improvement Take?

pages.title: Loan Against Property (LAP) — Complete Guide 2026
pages.description: Complete guide to Loan Against Property in India 2026. Learn about LAP eligibility, documents, interest rates, LTV ratio, and how LAP compares to personal loans.
pages.keywords: loan against property guide, LAP loan guide, loan against property eligibility, LAP vs personal loan, loan against property documents 2026
pages.heroSub: Everything you need to know about LAP: eligibility, documents, interest rates, LTV ratio, and when to choose it over a personal loan.

faqs.q: What is the maximum loan amount I can get against my property?
faqs.a: The maximum loan amount depends on your property's market value and the bank's Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio. Most banks offer 50–65% of the property's market value for residential properties and 40–55% for commercial properties. For example, if your property is valued at ₹1 Crore, you can typically get ₹50–65 Lakh as LAP.

faqs.q: Can I get a loan against property if the property is jointly owned?
faqs.a: Yes, but all co-owners of the property must be co-applicants or provide their consent and sign the loan agreement. The bank will require identity and address proof from all property owners, along with a joint declaration authorizing the mortgage.

faqs.q: What happens if I default on a loan against property?
faqs.a: If you default on LAP repayments, the bank will first send notices and try to restructure the loan. If you continue to default, the bank can initiate legal proceedings under the SARFAESI Act to seize and auction your property to recover the outstanding amount. This process typically takes 3–6 months after formal notice.

faqs.q: Can I take a loan against property that already has an existing home loan?
faqs.a: Yes, this is possible through a top-up loan or a second charge loan, but the combined LTV across both loans must remain within the bank's limits. Some banks allow a second mortgage if there is sufficient equity in the property after accounting for the outstanding home loan balance.

faqs.q: Is the interest paid on loan against property tax-deductible?
faqs.a: Tax benefits depend on the end-use of the loan. If the LAP funds are used for business purposes, the interest paid is deductible as a business expense. If used for home purchase or renovation, you can claim deduction under Section 24(b) up to ₹2 Lakh per year. No tax benefit is available if the funds are used for personal expenses.

guideContent.text: What Is a Loan Against Property (LAP)?

A Loan Against Property (LAP), also known as a mortgage loan, is a secured loan where you pledge your residential, commercial, or industrial property as collateral to borrow funds. Since the bank holds your property as security, LAP interest rates are significantly lower than unsecured loans like personal loans. You retain ownership and can continue to use the property while repaying the loan.

LAP is popular for funding large expenses such as business expansion, higher education, medical emergencies, debt consolidation, or even purchasing another property. Loan amounts typically range from ₹5 Lakh to ₹10 Crore, with tenures extending up to 15–20 years.
LAP Eligibility Criteria
Banks evaluate several factors when processing a LAP application. Meeting these criteria improves your chances of approval and may help you negotiate better terms.

items: Age: 25–65 years (at loan maturity). Some banks accept applicants from 21 years.
Income: Minimum ₹25,000–₹40,000 per month for salaried applicants. Self-employed applicants need minimum annual turnover of ₹10–15 Lakh.
CIBIL Score: 650 and above. A score of 750+ qualifies you for the best rates.
Property Ownership: The property must be in the applicant's name or jointly held (all owners must be co-applicants).
Property Age: The property should not be older than 30–40 years (varies by bank). Under-construction properties are generally not accepted.
Employment Stability: At least 2–3 years of total work experience for salaried applicants, or 3+ years of business existence for self-employed applicants.
Existing Obligations: Your total EMIs (including the proposed LAP) should not exceed 50–60% of your monthly income (FOIR limit).

guideContent.text: Documents Required for LAP
Identity and Address Proof

items: PAN Card (mandatory)
Aadhaar Card
Passport / Voter ID / Driving License
Passport-size photographs (2–4 copies)

guideContent.text: Income Documents (Salaried)

items: Salary slips (last 3 months)
Bank statements (last 6 months showing salary credits)
Form 16 or ITR (last 2 years)
Employment proof or appointment letter

guideContent.text: Income Documents (Self-Employed)

items: ITR with computation of income (last 3 years)
Audited balance sheet and P&L statement (last 3 years)
Business registration or GST certificate
Bank statements (last 12 months)

guideContent.text: Property Documents

items: Original title deed or sale deed
Encumbrance certificate (last 13–30 years)
Property tax receipts (paid up to date)
Approved building plan or layout plan
NOC from housing society (if applicable)
Property valuation report (bank may arrange independently)

guideContent.text: Interest Rates Overview

LAP interest rates in India currently range from 8.50% to 12.00% per annum, depending on the lender, your credit profile, and the type of property. Public sector banks like SBI and Bank of Baroda typically offer the lowest rates starting around 8.50–9.00%, while private banks and NBFCs may charge 9.50–12.00%. Most LAP loans come with floating interest rates linked to the bank's repo rate or MCLR.
LTV Ratio Explained
The Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio determines what percentage of your property's market value the bank will lend. This is a critical factor in how much you can borrow.

The actual LTV may be lower if the property is old, located in a non-preferred area, or has legal complications. Banks conduct an independent property valuation through approved assessors, and the loan amount is based on this assessed value, not the market price you expect.
LAP vs Personal Loan — Which Should You Choose?

Choose LAP when you need a large loan amount (above ₹5 Lakh) and can afford the longer processing time. The lower interest rate and extended tenure make EMIs very affordable. Choose a personal loan when you need quick disbursement for a smaller amount and do not want to put your property at risk.
Key Tips Before Applying for LAP

items: Get your property documents verified by a legal expert before approaching the bank. Title disputes can cause rejection.
Compare offers from at least 3–4 lenders. Even a 0.25% difference in interest rate saves lakhs over a long tenure.
Negotiate processing fees. Many banks are willing to reduce or waive processing fees for strong profiles.
Ensure the property has clear title and is free from any encumbrance or legal dispute.
Maintain a CIBIL score of 750+ before applying to get the best possible rate.
Calculate your FOIR before applying. If your total EMIs will exceed 50% of income, reduce existing debt first.

pages.title: How to Get Personal Loan Approved — Eligibility Tips 2026
pages.description: Expert tips to get your personal loan approved in 2026. Understand eligibility factors like income, CIBIL score, FOIR ratio, employer type, and what to do if rejected.
pages.keywords: personal loan eligibility tips, how to get personal loan approved, personal loan rejection reasons, increase personal loan eligibility, FOIR ratio personal loan 2026
pages.heroTitle: How to Get Personal Loan Approved
pages.heroSub: Understand the key eligibility factors banks check, tips to improve your chances, and what to do if your application gets rejected.

faqs.q: What is the minimum salary required for a personal loan?
faqs.a: Most banks require a minimum net monthly salary of ₹15,000–₹25,000 for personal loan eligibility. Some NBFCs accept applicants with salaries as low as ₹12,000. The actual eligible loan amount depends on your salary, existing EMIs, and the bank's FOIR limit.

faqs.q: Why was my personal loan application rejected?
faqs.a: Common rejection reasons include: CIBIL score below 700, high existing EMIs (FOIR above 50%), insufficient income, unstable employment history (less than 1 year at current job), too many recent loan inquiries, or employer not on the bank's approved list. Check your CIBIL report for errors and address the specific reason before reapplying.

faqs.q: How many times can I apply for a personal loan after rejection?
faqs.a: There is no hard limit, but each application triggers a hard inquiry that can lower your score by 5–10 points. Wait at least 3–6 months between applications. Use this time to improve your CIBIL score, reduce existing debt, and address the rejection reason.

faqs.q: Can I get a personal loan without salary slips?
faqs.a: Yes, if you are self-employed, banks accept ITR, bank statements, and business proof instead of salary slips. Some banks also offer personal loans based on strong bank account transaction history alone, even without formal income proof, though at higher interest rates.

faqs.q: Does my employer category affect personal loan eligibility?
faqs.a: Yes, significantly. Banks categorize employers into tiers — Category A (government, MNCs, Fortune 500), Category B (mid-size companies, listed firms), and Category C (small firms, startups). Category A employees get the highest loan amounts, lowest rates, and minimal documentation requirements.

guideContent.text: Key Eligibility Factors Banks Check

When you apply for a personal loan, banks run a multi-point evaluation to determine whether to approve your application and how much to offer. Understanding these factors puts you in a stronger position to secure approval at the best terms.
1. Monthly Income

Your net monthly income is the foundation of your eligibility. Banks typically require a minimum salary of ₹15,000–₹25,000 per month for salaried applicants. For self-employed individuals, annual income of ₹3–5 Lakh (as reflected in ITR) is usually the baseline. Higher income directly translates to higher eligible loan amounts because banks allow EMIs only up to a fixed percentage of your income.
2. CIBIL Score

Your CIBIL score is the most critical factor after income. Most banks set 700 as the minimum threshold for personal loans, while premium offers (lowest rates, highest amounts) are reserved for applicants with 750+. A score below 650 will result in rejection from most banks, though some NBFCs may still approve at higher interest rates (16–24% p.a.).
3. Employer Type and Category
Banks classify employers into categories that directly impact your loan terms:

rows: Highest amount, lowest rates, minimal docs
Mid-size firms, listed companies, reputed private cos
Lower amount, higher rates, more docs required

guideContent.text: If your employer is in Category A, you may receive pre-approved offers directly from your bank without even applying. Category C employees often need to provide additional income proof and may face higher interest rates.
4. Existing EMIs and Debt

Banks look at your existing monthly obligations including all running EMIs (home loan, car loan, other personal loans) and credit card minimum dues. The total of your existing EMIs plus the proposed new EMI must stay within the bank's FOIR (Fixed Obligation to Income Ratio) limit.
FOIR Ratio Explained

FOIR stands for Fixed Obligation to Income Ratio. It is the percentage of your monthly income that goes toward paying fixed obligations like EMIs. Banks typically allow a maximum FOIR of 40–55% for personal loans.

For example, if your monthly salary is ₹50,000 and the bank's FOIR limit is 50%, your maximum total EMI capacity is ₹25,000. If you already pay ₹10,000 in existing EMIs, only ₹15,000 is available for the new personal loan EMI. At 12% interest for 3 years, this translates to an eligible loan amount of approximately ₹4.5 Lakh.
Tips to Increase Your Personal Loan Eligibility
1. Improve Your CIBIL Score Before Applying

If your score is below 750, spend 3–6 months improving it before applying. Pay all bills on time, reduce credit card utilization below 30%, and avoid new credit inquiries. Even a 50-point improvement can significantly reduce your interest rate and increase the approved amount.
2. Close or Reduce Existing Loans

Paying off smaller outstanding loans (like a bike loan or an old personal loan) frees up FOIR capacity and makes you eligible for a larger amount. Even making a partial prepayment on existing loans reduces your EMI burden and improves eligibility.
3. Add a Co-Applicant or Guarantor

Some banks allow a co-applicant (usually a spouse or parent) whose income is combined with yours for eligibility. This can substantially increase the loan amount you qualify for. A guarantor with a strong credit profile can also help if your own profile is borderline.
4. Choose the Right Bank Based on Your Profile

Do not apply randomly to multiple banks. If your employer is in Category A, apply to banks where you hold a salary account for pre-approved offers. If your CIBIL score is average (650–700), consider NBFCs like Bajaj Finserv or Tata Capital that have more flexible criteria. Targeted applications reduce hard inquiries on your report.
5. Opt for a Longer Tenure

Choosing a 5-year tenure instead of 3 years reduces your monthly EMI significantly, which means the bank's FOIR calculation allows a higher loan amount. While you will pay more total interest, this strategy maximizes your approved amount if that is the priority.
6. Show Additional Income Sources

If you have rental income, freelance earnings, or investment income, provide supporting documentation. Some banks consider 50–100% of additional income when calculating eligibility. Bank statements showing regular additional credits strengthen your application.
What to Do If Your Personal Loan Is Rejected

items: Request the specific rejection reason from the bank. Under RBI guidelines, banks must provide the reason upon request.
Check your CIBIL report immediately. If the rejection was due to credit score, identify and fix the issues (errors, overdue accounts, high utilization).
Wait at least 3–6 months before reapplying. Use this time to improve your profile.
Consider alternative options: loan against FD, gold loan, or loan against mutual funds offer lower rates and easier approval.
Try a different lender. If a bank rejected you, an NBFC with more flexible criteria might approve your application.
Reduce the loan amount. Sometimes applying for a smaller amount that fits well within your FOIR can result in approval.
